sections.title: What conjunctions & linkers do (core logic)
sections.content:
Conjunctions and linkers are the **explicit machinery** that joins ideas — the Clause Linking root (#4 in the Tree Model) made visible.

Without them, writing reads as a list of disconnected statements. At B2 — essays, emails, exam Writing — the examiner reads specifically for these connectives; in speech, they signal where your thought is going next.

left.title: Conjunctions
points: link words, phrases or clauses **inside** a sentence

diagrams.title: Key principle: choose in this order

items.label: 2. Grammar slot
items.formula: clause-internal conjunction / sentence-initial linker / fronted dependent clause

diagrams.label: Trap
diagrams.text: Mixing the slots produces the classic double-linking error.

sections.title: Coordinating conjunctions — FANBOYS
sections.content: **Coordinating conjunctions** — the workhorses of every sentence — link **equal-rank** elements: word + word, phrase + phrase, or clause + clause.

items.good: I was tired**,** but I kept working.
items.why: two independent clauses → comma before the conjunction

items.good: It was late**,** so we went home.
Hurry up**,** or you'll miss the train.

items.why: two words or short phrases → no comma

diagrams.text: Czech doesn't always require the comma before *but / so / or* in this pattern — in English written work, leave it in.

sections.title: Subordinating conjunctions
sections.content: **Subordinating conjunctions** introduce a **dependent clause** — one that cannot stand alone — packing background, condition and timing into one sentence.

items.label: Dependent clause first → comma
items.formula: Although it rained, we went out.

items.label: Main clause first → no comma
items.formula: We went out although it rained.

items.good: Although it rained, we went out.
items.why: a subordinating clause is not a sentence on its own — fragment

sections.title: Contrast linkers — but / although / however / despite
sections.content:
Four ways to mark contrast, **four different grammar slots**. Getting the slot wrong is the #1 source of comma errors at B1-B2.

The most-tested linking function in FCE/CAE Sentence Transformation and Open Cloze. *Despite* also takes a plain noun: **Despite** my tiredness, I kept working.

diagrams.title: Contrast linkers — structure side by side
rows: I was tired, but I kept going.
Although I was tired, I kept going.
I was tired. However, I kept going.
Despite being tired, I kept going.

sections.title: Cause & result — because / so / therefore / due to
sections.content:
Two families: **cause** (the reason) and **result** (the consequence). Same logical relationship, different sentence positions.

Essays and exam tasks constantly require these — mixing them up, or doubling them (*because … so …*), is one of the most visible learner errors.

diagrams.title: Cause vs result — choose by slot and register

items.good: **Since** the trains were cancelled, we drove.
items.why: cause + clause — *since / as* are more formal

items.good: **Due to** the rain, we stayed in.

sections.title: Addition & sequence — also / furthermore / first / finally
sections.content:
**Addition linkers** add a second idea; **sequence linkers** organise paragraphs in order. Register is the lever.

At B2 your essays need to **flow**, not list — choosing *furthermore* over *also* (or vice versa) shows the examiner you're controlling register, not just stringing facts together.

diagrams.title: Addition — from informal to formal
rows: He's a teacher. He **also** writes novels.
He's a teacher. **In addition,** he writes novels.
The plan is cheap. **Furthermore,** it's quick to implement.
**Not only** is it cheap, **but** it's **also** fast.

diagrams.label: Trap
diagrams.text: *At last* means "after a long wait" — don't use it to mean *finally* in a list.

sections.title: Discourse markers (B2) — formal emphasis, contrast, restatement
sections.content:
Sentence-level signals that don't fit the cause / contrast / addition boxes — they guide the reader through the **shape** of the argument.

They carry no new information — they tell the listener what kind of move you're making next. These are the B2-to-C1 differentiators in writing and spoken fluency.

diagrams.title: Key markers by function
rows: The meeting was short. **In fact,** it lasted ten minutes.
*on the other hand, in contrast, conversely*
Cars are fast. **On the other hand,** they pollute.
*in other words, that is to say, i.e.*
I saw Tom yesterday. **By the way,** he's moving to Brno.

diagrams.text: Many of these don't translate one-to-one from Czech. *In fact* is **not** *v faktu*. Learn them as ready-made chunks.

sections.title: Common Czech-speaker errors
sections.content:
**High-frequency mistakes** at B1-B2 — most are slot errors, not vocabulary errors.
- Over-use of *so* in writing: spoken English uses *so* freely; written/formal English prefers *therefore, as a result, consequently*.

All of these appear in Use of English Open Cloze and Sentence Transformation, and they're the first things a reader notices in a CV cover letter or business email. Fixing them gives an immediate visible jump in apparent level.

items.good: **Although** I was tired, I kept working. / I was tired, **but** I kept working.
items.bad: Although I was tired, but I kept working.
items.why: double linking — the biggest one; same for *because … so …* and *despite … but …*

items.good: I was tired, **but** I kept working.
items.bad: I was tired, I kept working.
items.why: comma splice — a comma alone cannot join two clauses; use a conjunction, a full stop, or a semicolon

items.good: **Despite** the rain / **In spite of** the rain
It was cold. **However,** we went out.
items.bad: It was cold, however we went out.
items.why: *however* needs a new sentence, or at minimum a semicolon

questions.question: Which is correct?

options.a: Because it rained, we stayed at home.
options.b: Because it rained, so we stayed at home.
options.c: Because it rained, therefore we stayed.
options.d: It rained, because we stayed at home.

questions.explanation: Don't use because and so (or therefore) in the same sentence. Use one: Because it rained, we stayed. Or: It rained so we stayed.

questions.question: Which is correct?

options.a: Although he tried, but he failed.
options.b: Although he tried, however he failed.
options.d: Although he tried, and he failed.

questions.explanation: Don't use although and but (or however) together. Use one: Although he tried, he failed. Or: He tried but he failed.

questions.question: Which linker fits? 'It was expensive. _____, we bought it.'
questions.explanation: However shows contrast (expensive vs we bought it). Therefore would mean 'because it was expensive we bought it' which is odd.

questions.question: Which fits? 'I'll call you _____ I arrive.'
questions.explanation: When = at the time (I arrive). While is for two ongoing actions; during needs a noun (during the meeting).

questions.question: Which fits? 'She likes tea, _____ he prefers coffee.' (contrast)
questions.explanation: Whereas (or while) compares two contrasting facts inside one sentence: She likes tea, whereas he prefers coffee.

questions.question: Which fits? 'I don't know _____ she agreed.'

options.d: Both a and b are correct.

questions.explanation: Whether and if can both introduce an indirect yes/no question (I don't know whether/if she agreed). Whether is often preferred in formal writing.

questions.question: Wrong: 'Because it was late, so we went home.' Correct the sentence.
answers: Because it was late, we went home.
It was late so we went home.
Because it was late, we went home
It was late so we went home
questions.explanation: Don't use because and so together. Use one or the other.

questions.question: Wrong: 'Although she was tired, but she continued.' Correct the sentence.
answers: Although she was tired, she continued.
She was tired but she continued.
Although she was tired, she continued
She was tired but she continued
questions.explanation: Don't use although and but together.

questions.question: Fill in: It was difficult. _____, we managed to finish. (contrast)
answers: However
questions.explanation: However shows contrast (difficult vs we managed).

questions.question: Fill in: He didn't study. _____, he failed the exam. (result)
answers: Therefore

questions.question: Fill in: I was reading _____ she was cooking. (at the same time)
answers: while

questions.question: Wrong: 'Because he was ill, therefore he stayed in bed.' Correct the sentence.
answers: Because he was ill, he stayed in bed.
He was ill so he stayed in bed.
Because he was ill, he stayed in bed
He was ill so he stayed in bed
questions.explanation: Don't use because and therefore together.

questions.question: Fill in: We need to hurry _____ we miss the train. (negative purpose / result to avoid)
answers: or
otherwise
or we will
otherwise we will
questions.explanation: Or (we'll) miss = otherwise we'll miss. Shows negative result to avoid.

questions.question: Fill in: The film was long. _____, it was very good.
answers: However

questions.question: Fill in: _____ you see him, tell him to call me.
answers: When
questions.explanation: When = at the time you see him.

questions.question: Fill in: I don't know _____ she's coming. (introducing a yes/no alternative – one word)
answers: whether
if
questions.explanation: Whether and if can both introduce an indirect yes/no question (whether/if she's coming or not).

questions.question: Fill in: _____ it was expensive, we decided to buy it. (contrast)
answers: Although
questions.explanation: Although introduces a contrast (expensive vs we bought it).
